title: Noun Gender and Definite Articles
description: Learn how Bulgarian nouns work with gender, definite forms, and a few common patterns.

content.title: Why Gender Matters
content.description: Bulgarian nouns are usually masculine, feminine, or neuter, and the form of the article depends on that gender.

content.title: Big idea
content.text: Unlike English, Bulgarian usually puts the definite article after the noun: ученикът, книгата, морето.

content.title: What to watch for
bullets: Learn the noun together with its gender.
Check the ending, but do not rely on the ending alone.
Practice the definite form as a whole word, not as a separate article.

content.title: Common Gender Patterns
content.description: These are helpful patterns, but exceptions do exist.

content.title: Definite Forms
content.description: Bulgarian adds the article to the end of the noun.

rows: the city / the city as object

content.title: Useful detail
content.text: Some masculine nouns use different definite forms depending on whether they are the subject or object in the sentence.

content.title: Sentence Examples
content.description: See gender and definite forms inside simple full sentences.

content.note: The noun is definite because the listener knows which teacher is meant.

content.en: The book is on the table.
content.note: A definite feminine noun ends in -та.
Neuter nouns often take -то in the definite form.

content.title: Study steps
items: Say the noun with the article out loud.
Try to remember the gender when you see a new word.
Use the noun in a full sentence, not only a vocabulary list.

content.question: Which noun form is definite?
content.note: The ending -та is a strong clue for many feminine nouns.
